title_en: Taharah in Nayl al-Awtar: Hadith Evidence for Purification
content_en:
Ash-Shawkani's treatment of taharah in Nayl al-Awtar approaches purification law as a hadith scholar — presenting the primary texts, assessing their authenticity, and then deriving the rulings from them with attention to how the four schools have interpreted the same hadiths differently. This approach makes the taharah chapters an excellent introduction to the evidential debates underlying the schools' disagreements.

The first major hadith ash-Shawkani discusses is the foundational report establishing the purifying nature of water: 'Verily, water is pure and nothing makes it impure' (Abu Dawud, an-Nasa'i, at-Tirmidhi). He assesses this hadith as authentically established and derives from it the baseline principle that water is presumed pure and purifying until specific evidence establishes otherwise. This principle, accepted by all four schools, becomes the foundation for all subsequent water classification discussions.

On the two-qullah threshold, ash-Shawkani presents the relevant hadith (Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidhi) and discusses its chain of transmission in detail. He notes that some hadith scholars have questioned aspects of its chain while others have declared it acceptable, and that ash-Shafi'i accepted it as the basis for his school's distinction between small and large quantities of water. Ash-Shawkani himself leans toward the view that the hadith is sufficient to establish this distinction, giving the Shafi'i position on water law a stronger evidential basis than some critics allow.

The chapter on the purifying function of seawater features the famous hadith of Abu Hurairah: when asked by a man about performing wudu with seawater on sea voyages, the Prophet replied: 'Its water is pure, and its dead are lawful' (Malik in al-Muwatta', Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidhi, an-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah). Ash-Shawkani assesses this hadith as sahih (authentic) and notes that all four schools accept it as establishing the purifying nature of seawater, but that it also establishes the permissibility of consuming sea creatures — a ruling the Hanafi school partially restricts through its own reasoning.

The hadiths on wudu are treated with ash-Shawkani's attention to variant narrations. The description of the Prophet's wudu in Sahih al-Bukhari (narrated by 'Uthman ibn 'Affan) is taken as the primary model: washing the face three times, washing the hands and arms three times, wiping the head once, and washing the feet three times. From this description and the Quranic text (5:6), ash-Shawkani derives the obligatory elements and discusses where the schools differ in their reading of the same sources.

The nullifiers of wudu chapter showcases ash-Shawkani's comparative method particularly well. For each claimed nullifier, he presents the supporting hadith evidence, assesses the hadith's authenticity, presents the positions of the schools, and then identifies what he considers the most evidentially supported position. On the Hanbali position that eating camel meat nullifies wudu, he cites Sahih Muslim's narration from Jabir ibn Samurah (the Prophet commanded wudu after eating camel meat) and assesses it as sahih, concluding that this ruling — adopted by the Hanbali school and by some scholars of other schools — is strongly evidentially grounded.

On the Shafi'i position that touching a woman nullifies wudu, ash-Shawkani presents the competing hadiths: those that support touching as a nullifier and those narrated from 'A'ishah indicating the Prophet did not perform wudu after kissing his wife before prayer. He concludes that the hadiths indicating touching alone (without sexual pleasure) does not nullify wudu are stronger, aligning with the Hanafi and Hanbali positions against the Shafi'i.
title_id: Taharah dalam Nayl al-Awtar: Bukti Hadis untuk Bersuci
content_id:
Pembahasan asy-Syawkani tentang taharah dalam Nayl al-Awtar mendekati hukum bersuci sebagaimana seorang ahli hadis — menyajikan teks-teks utama, menilai keshahihannya, kemudian mengambil hukum dari teks tersebut dengan memperhatikan bagaimana mazhab empat menafsirkan hadis yang sama secara berbeda. Pendekatan ini menjadikan bab-bab taharah sebagai pengantar yang sangat baik untuk perdebatan evidensial yang mendasari perbedaan pendapat antar mazhab.

Hadis utama pertama yang dibahas asy-Syawkani adalah laporan pokok yang menetapkan sifat menyucikan air: 'Sesungguhnya air itu suci dan tidak ada sesuatu pun yang menajiskannya' (Abu Dawud, an-Nasa'i, at-Tirmidzi). Beliau menilai hadis ini sebagai terbukti shahih dan darinya mengambil prinsip dasar bahwa air dianggap suci dan menyucikan sampai ada bukti khusus yang menetapkan sebaliknya. Prinsip ini, yang diterima oleh semua mazhab empat, menjadi fondasi bagi semua pembahasan klasifikasi air selanjutnya.

Mengenai ambang batas dua qullah, asy-Syawkani menyajikan hadis yang relevan (Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidzi) dan mendiskusikan sanadnya secara terperinci. Beliau mencatat bahwa sebagian ulama hadis mempertanyakan aspek-aspek tertentu dari sanadnya sementara yang lain menyatakannya dapat diterima, dan bahwa asy-Syafi'i menerimanya sebagai dasar bagi pembedaan mazhab beliau antara kuantitas air yang sedikit dan banyak. Asy-Syawkani sendiri cenderung ke pandangan bahwa hadis ini cukup untuk menetapkan pembedaan ini, memberikan dasar evidensial yang lebih kuat bagi posisi mazhab Syafi'i tentang hukum air daripada yang diakui sebagian kritikus.

Bab tentang fungsi menyucikan air laut menampilkan hadis terkenal dari Abu Hurairah ra: ketika seorang lelaki bertanya tentang berwudu dengan air laut di perjalanan laut, Nabi ﷺ menjawab: 'Airnya suci, dan bangkainya halal' (Malik dalam al-Muwatta', Abu Dawud, at-Tirmidzi, an-Nasa'i, Ibn Majah). Asy-Syawkani menilai hadis ini sebagai shahih dan mencatat bahwa semua mazhab empat menerimanya sebagai dalil yang menetapkan sifat menyucikan air laut.
